steps.title: Identify what controls margin of error
steps.content: The margin of error for a proportion estimate is $$\text{MOE} = z^* \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$$, where $$n$$ is the sample size. The MOE is inversely proportional to $$\sqrt{n}$$: larger samples produce smaller margins of error.

steps.title: Determine the effect of doubling n
steps.content:
The sample size doubles from 144 to 288. The new MOE scales by the factor:
$$\frac{\text{MOE}_{\text{new}}}{\text{MOE}_{\text{old}}} = \sqrt{\frac{144}{288}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} \approx 0.707$$
New MOE $\approx 5.40\% \times 0.707 \approx 3.82\%$, which is lower.

steps.title: Note that the point estimate is unaffected
steps.content: Increasing sample size makes the estimate more precise (smaller MOE), but does not systematically shift the estimate itself up or down. The 84% figure is expected to remain approximately the same.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices
steps.content:
• "The margin of error will be lower.": The margin of error will be lower -- correct; larger sample shrinks MOE.
  • "The margin of error will be higher.": The margin of error will be higher -- wrong; larger $$n$$ reduces MOE, not increases it. Eliminated.
  • "The estimate of the percentage of the fitness center's members that would continue to pay for a membership if the price increased will be lower.": The estimate will be lower -- wrong; sample size does not bias the estimate downward. Eliminated.
  • "The estimate of the percentage of the fitness center's members that would continue to pay for a membership if the price increased will be higher.": The estimate will be higher -- wrong; same reasoning, the estimate is not systematically shifted. Eliminated.
That matches the correct answer -- A is correct.
